skill.name: payoff-action-modeling
skill.description: Framework for modeling product UI actions after a user achieves a meaningful outcome. Helps teams decide which actions to show, hide, group, name, prioritize, and place across outcome, item, selection, continuation, navigation, recovery, and assistance scopes.
skill.summary: Structured framework for designing post-outcome UI action models from user intent questions.
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: All 142 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is a pure documentation guide for UX/UI product design. Backtick characters flagged as 'shell execution' are standard Markdown inline code formatting for UI action labels. Findings flagged as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' are markdown table content, YAML frontmatter, and UX guidance text with no cryptographic content. The single URL reference to casely.digital is a passive documentation mention, not executable network code. No executable code, data exfiltration, command injection, or environmental access was found.

low_findings.title: External URL reference in documentation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 298 contains a URL to casely.digital in a documentation note. This is a passive reference suggesting a hosted tool, not an executable network request. The URL is clearly documented as an optional mention. No data transmission or credential exposure risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URL is a documentation reference with no code execution context. No security risk but noted for transparency.

content.user_title: Design UI action models from user intent questions
content.value_statement: Product teams struggle to decide what UI actions to show after a user completes a task. This skill provides a structured framework to model actions from user intent, placing them at the right scope and priority level.
actual_capabilities: Classify outcome states into completion, generation, workspace, review, handoff, recovery, and continuation types
Generate 12 to 25 user intent questions from realistic post-outcome scenarios
Assign action scope and intent pressure to every UI action
Map actions to correct UI placement from primary CTA to contextual menus
Distinguish one-item, selected-item, and full-result action labels clearly
Review action models against density, locality, and momentum principles
limitations: Does not generate visual UI designs, wireframes, or mockups
Requires domain knowledge of the specific product being modeled
Does not replace user research or usability testing
The framework may need adaptation for non-standard or highly specialized workflows

use_cases.title: Design post-onboarding action screens
use_cases.description: Use the framework to decide which actions to show after a user completes onboarding, setup, or first-time configuration. Avoid overwhelming new users while providing clear next steps.

use_cases.title: Plan dashboard action hierarchies
use_cases.description: Organize actions in a dashboard or workspace by classifying them into outcome, selection, item, navigation, and recovery scopes with appropriate urgency levels.

use_cases.title: Review existing interfaces for action clarity
use_cases.description: Audit an existing UI against outcome modeling principles to identify duplicated actions, vague labels, and competing primary CTAs. Produce a concrete improvement plan.

prompt_templates.title: Basic outcome action model
prompt_templates.prompt: I just built a file upload feature for my app. After a user uploads a file, I need to model what actions to show on the completion screen. Use the outcome action modeling framework to produce a simple action model table with scope, pressure, and placement for at least 8 actions.
prompt_templates.scenario: A product designer needs a quick action model for a simple completion state.

prompt_templates.title: Full dashboard action model
prompt_templates.prompt: I am designing a project management dashboard that appears after a user creates a new project. The dashboard shows tasks, team members, and recent activity. Use the full outcome action modeling framework to classify the outcome state, generate at least 15 user intent questions, and produce an action table with all scopes, pressures, and placements.
prompt_templates.scenario: A product manager needs a complete action model for a multi-scope workspace.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-scope workflow with edge cases
prompt_templates.prompt: I am designing a data import flow where users upload a CSV, map fields, review results, and handle errors. The post-import screen needs to support retry failed items, download error logs, approve mapped rows, and export results. Use the framework with special attention to recovery actions, ambiguous scope boundaries, and intent pressure conflicts. Add edge case handling for partial failures.
prompt_templates.scenario: A senior product designer needs to model actions for a complex workflow with error states.

prompt_templates.title: Review and optimize existing action model
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an existing action model for a content management system publish screen. The current model has 12 actions all shown at the same level. Use the framework to review this model for density issues, scope ambiguity, label clarity, and momentum problems. Identify at least 5 specific issues and propose a revised action table.
prompt_templates.scenario: A product lead wants to audit and improve an existing UI action hierarchy.

output_examples.input: After a user imports customer data from a CSV file, what actions should the UI show?
output: Outcome state type: handoff with review
Contextual actions: Map field (per column), Fix row (per error), Download error file
Continuation branches: Import more, Start new import
Recovery actions: Undo import, View import history
Placement notes: Error rows show Fix inline, success summary at top with Review CTA

output_examples.input: Our file upload completion screen shows Download, Share, Delete, Rename, Move, Copy link, and Add description all at the same level. What is wrong with this?
output: Issue 1: No primary CTA - 7 actions at equal visual weight creates decision fatigue
Issue 2: Download should be primary CTA (highest-value next action)
Issue 3: Delete is destructive and should be contextual with recovery path, not at top level
Issue 4: Move and Copy link are deferred actions that can go in a secondary menu
Issue 5: Add description is a refinement action, not an outcome action
Proposed: Primary CTA: Download | Secondary: Share, Copy link | Menu: Rename, Move | Contextual with confirm: Delete

best_practices: Start by defining the outcome state type before placing any actions. This determines the entire action hierarchy.
Keep one clear primary CTA per outcome screen. Multiple primary-level actions create decision fatigue.
Use concrete action labels that describe user outcomes rather than implementation details.
anti_patterns: Do not overload first-success screens with every possible action. Defer advanced features to secondary menus.
Avoid treating all actions as equal in visual hierarchy. Use intent pressure to differentiate immediate, contextual, and deferred actions.
Do not hide important recovery actions like undo or retry behind generic menus. Place them near the state they recover from.

faq.question: What types of product screens does this framework work for?
faq.answer: This framework works for any screen where a user has achieved a meaningful outcome. This includes completion states, generation results, workspaces, review screens, handoff flows, recovery states, and continuation branches.

faq.question: How many actions should a single screen show?
faq.answer: There is no fixed number, but the framework recommends one clear primary CTA and grouped contextual actions. Avoid showing more than 5 to 7 visible actions at once. Defer less urgent actions to secondary menus.

faq.question: Does this framework replace user research?
faq.answer: No. The framework provides a structured starting point based on common UI patterns. It should be validated with user testing and domain-specific research.

faq.question: What is the difference between outcome scope and item scope?
faq.answer: Outcome scope actions affect the full result, such as Download all or Publish all. Item scope actions affect one specific item, such as Edit or Delete on a single row. These scopes must have distinct labels and placements.

faq.question: How do I handle destructive actions in the model?
faq.answer: Destructive actions should be contextual and paired with recovery paths. Place them near the object they affect, not as primary CTAs. Always include confirmation, undo, or archive functionality.

faq.question: Can I use this framework for mobile UI design?
faq.answer: Yes, but adapt placement guidelines for smaller screens. Mobile layouts may require more actions behind menus or bottom sheets. The scope and pressure classification still applies.
